posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
As we all know , 1982 was a breakthrough vintage for Bordeaux and for Robert Parker and a couple of other young wine writers like Michel Bettane . The vintage was not praised by the old guard , finding the wines too easy and jammy . They were wrong .
Of course , after 35 years , the wines are now loosing some freshness and fat . But I found them still extremely elegant with amazing sweetness and secundary aromas like cedar and tobacco dominating . Most wines came from our cellars , bought on release but not all of them . Not all of the bottles were at their best but that is very normal . The winner was the only magnum in the tasting and it is not surprising that we preferred the wine in magnum . Wine in magnum does age better , there is no doubt about it in my mind .

wine 1 . Very dark , murky , this is not a great bottle , of balance . The wine improves after 1 hour but is not at it’s best . 92 ? ( Mouton R. )
wine 2 : very sweet , fresh , tobacco and cedar , elegant , my wotn , amazing elegance . 98/100 ( Pichon Comtesse , magnum )
wine 3 : over ripe , tomatoes smell , this is not good NR ( La Violette … too bad , my friend who brought it claimed he has superb bottles before )
wine 4 : opulent and flashy , blue fruit , low acid , jammy , a crowd pleaser , at it’s peak 96/100 ( Latour )
wine 5 : fresh , dark color , red fruit , spicy , lovely wine 95/100 ( Cheval Blanc )
wine 6 : very elegant , earthy , still fresh and not fading , spicy , pure elegance , not a power wine , love it 95/100 ( Magdeleine , a nice surprise )
wine 7 : elegant and spicy , not the greatest concentration but all well in place , blue fruit , 95/100 ( Petrus )
wine 8 : very intense nose , most concentrated wine of the evening , big and powerful , Parker wine , 97/100 ( Lafleur )
wine 9 : elegant and sweet , cedar , slightly less complex than the others but excellent wine 94/100 ( Leoville Poyferre )

wine 10 : Yquem 1982 : not blind : I love this Yquem , much beter than it’s reputation ( I prefer it over 83 , 85 , 86 and 89 ; maybe not 88 ) , all well in place with good acidities .

If you have a lot of 1982’s , drink them , they are at their peak or slightly fading . If you like power , drink them . If you like secundary and even tertiary aroma’s , you have plenty of time especially from magnum .

posts.name: Victor Hong
posts.cooked: I am glad still to have the Leoville Poyferre.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Herwig Janssen:

The vintage was not praised by the old guard , finding the wines too easy and jammy . They were wrong .

This is a myth. I remember reading articles in Decanter magazine written by the old guard highly praising the vintage (although of course using much more understated language than did Robert Parker). It was certain establishment US writers that trashed the vintage - most prominently Robert Finnegan and Terry Robards who trashed the vintage. But many of the famous British writers had good things to say.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: Nice group of wines. I don’t think Mouton has even come close to peaking yet. Latour and Pichon are always in my top 2 when drinking top '82s. I don’t think I’ve ever tasted the Lafleur.
heck of a tasting lineup!

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:
Herwig Janssen:

The vintage was not praised by the old guard , finding the wines too easy and jammy . They were wrong .

This is a myth. I remember reading articles in Decanter magazine written by the old guard highly praising the vintage (although of course using much more understated language than did Robert Parker). It was certain establishment US writers that trashed the vintage - most prominently Robert Finnegan and Terry Robards who trashed the vintage. But many of the famous British writers had good things to say.

I agree with you about the Brits; they really liked the vintage, although I think it is fair to say they did not go all in as Parker did

Terry Robards did not trash the vintage; he kind of liked it, but did not love it. But his big error was to steer people to the more affordable 1981s.

Planning a 1982 Tasting in June, but my experience of the vintage from random bottles has been incredibly good. Recently drunk Prieure Lichine, Branaire, Gruaud Larose and Figeac, all superb. Perhaps the biggest surprise was a Poujeaux from magnum which was as good as all the above, except for the Gruaud.

posts.name: Glenn Bienstock
posts.cooked: 82 Poujeaux was my Bordeaux epiphany in about 1990. It holds a special place in my heart.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Which do you think has the greatest potential for the future, Herwig? To my palate, it would definitely be the Lafleur.

I’m always surprised by quite how atypical the 1982 Latour is.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: Great tasting, thanks for the notes Herwig. As someone who began collecting Bordeaux a few decades after the ‘82s were released I’ve had only a modest sample. But - they’ve been very good. A 1er you missed was the Haut Brion, which I’ve found to be excellent. If you’ve had it at all recently, how do you think it compares to the group you tasted in terms of aging?

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
The greatest potential : no doubt , that must be the Lafleur . Massive and fresh . Then the Cheval Blanc I think and maybe a good bottle of Mouton . Don’t know about Haut Brion .
Indeed , Latour is very atypical .
I always loved the Gruaud Laroze .

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Nicely done, Herwig!

Curious from you guys, Herwig, William, Howard, et al, which subsequent vintages, if any, and in your humble opinion, compare or comes close to comparing to 1982 in terms of approachability, aging potential and breadth? What has always stricken me about 1982 is that most Chateau have been accessible for a very decent period of time, and still have more to go. The other thing is, the breadth of quality of this vintage, where lower tier or mid-tier Chateaux produced excellent wines, some their best, think Meyney, Sociando, La Louviere, La Lagune, etc.

posts.name: Lawrence Leichtman
posts.cooked: Had the 1982 Haut Brion in February. Nowhere near peak. Superb wine A+ on my scale.

posts.name: Gary Rust
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Nicely done, Herwig!

Curious from you guys, Herwig, William, Howard, et al, which subsequent vintages, if any, and in your humble opinion, compare or comes close to comparing to 1982 in terms of approachability, aging potential and breadth? What has always stricken me about 1982 is that most Chateau have been accessible for a very decent period of time, and still have more to go. The other thing is, the breadth of quality of this vintage, where lower tier or mid-tier Chateaux produced excellent wines, some their best, think Meyney, Sociando, La Louviere, La Lagune, etc.

I would say 1990 – different wines than 82s, but equally accessible early, excellent across the board (save for PLL, Mouton, a few others), and top wines still getting better.

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked: Thanks for the notes. That ‘82 Pichon was the magical bottle that got me hooked on wine, still saving a magnum.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Great report Herwig. Sorry to hear about the 82 Mouton. A bottle opened last month was just terrific. Glad to hear your Pichon Lalande was still going strong. A long-time favorite, some bottles are starting to show a little tired.

Robert, I agree with Gary Rust: 1990 is the vintage I find most similar to 1982. I’m not sure it’s been replicated since things turned more modern in the latter half of the 90s. Perhaps 2009 and 2015 are the most 82-ish of the recent vintages but only in relative terms of it being riper than other recent vintages. 2009 and 2015 are softer/riper/more modern than 1982.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Nicely done, Herwig!

Curious from you guys, Herwig, William, Howard, et al, which subsequent vintages, if any, and in your humble opinion, compare or comes close to comparing to 1982 in terms of approachability, aging potential and breadth? What has always stricken me about 1982 is that most Chateau have been accessible for a very decent period of time, and still have more to go. The other thing is, the breadth of quality of this vintage, where lower tier or mid-tier Chateaux produced excellent wines, some their best, think Meyney, Sociando, La Louviere, La Lagune, etc.

Not at all sure and totally different style but I have liked a number of 2005s a lot. And, having tasted exactly one 2016 (Pichon Lalande), I wonder about 2016.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Which do you think has the greatest potential for the future, Herwig? To my palate, it would definitely be the Lafleur.

I’m always surprised by quite how atypical the 1982 Latour is.

Last year, I had the Leoville las Cases twice and after years upon years in slumber this is really good.

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:
Robert Alfert:

Nicely done, Herwig!

Curious from you guys, Herwig, William, Howard, et al, which subsequent vintages, if any, and in your humble opinion, compare or comes close to comparing to 1982 in terms of approachability, aging potential and breadth? What has always stricken me about 1982 is that most Chateau have been accessible for a very decent period of time, and still have more to go. The other thing is, the breadth of quality of this vintage, where lower tier or mid-tier Chateaux produced excellent wines, some their best, think Meyney, Sociando, La Louviere, La Lagune, etc.

Not at all sure and totally different style but I have liked a number of 2005s a lot. And, having tasted exactly one 2016 (Pichon Lalande), I wonder about 2016.

The 2016 Pichon Lalande has gotten so much praise that I’ve come close several times to just buying the damn futures. Part of me doesn’t believe it could be that good, but still.
